title: Bucatini all'amatriciana
subtitle: Roman pasta with guanciale, tomato, and pecorino Romano
excerpt: Amatriciana sauce built on guanciale rendered in its own fat, tinned tomatoes cooked down with white wine and chilli, finished with sharp pecorino Romano and tossed through thick bucatini. One of Rome's four canonical pasta dishes, with origins in the mountain town of Amatrice.
sourceNotes: Amatriciana sauce originates in Amatrice, Lazio, and is documented in Italian cookery literature from the eighteenth century. The tomato-based version replaced an earlier white sauce without tomato during the nineteenth century. Multiple Italian regional cookery sources; no single public-domain text.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. Cook the pasta fresh.

content.text: Amatriciana sauce has three components and the balance between them is the dish. The guanciale provides salt and fat; the tomatoes provide acidity and body; the pecorino provides sharpness. Nothing else goes into the base. The Amatriciani are specific about the ingredients: guanciale, not pancetta; pecorino Romano, not Parmesan; bucatini or rigatoni, not spaghetti.

Render the guanciale over medium heat until the fat has run clear and the meat has begun to colour but not crisped all the way through. The rendered fat stays in the pan and becomes part of the sauce. Deglaze with white wine, let it evaporate fully, then add the tomatoes and cook for 15 to 20 minutes. The pasta goes into the sauce a minute before it is fully cooked, with a splash of pasta water to bring the sauce together into something glossy rather than oily.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{guanciale}} of guanciale and cook, stirring occasionally, for 8 to 10 minutes until the fat has rendered and the meat is lightly golden at the edges. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute, then remove and discard the garlic. Stir in the {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes and cook for 30 seconds.

Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and cook, scraping the pan, until the wine has fully evaporated. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Season with a little salt. Simmer over medium-low heat for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has reduced and thickened.

Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta in well-salted boiling water until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a large ladleful of pasta water, then drain. Add the pasta to the sauce with a splash of pasta water and toss over high heat for 1 to 2 minutes until the sauce coats the pasta and looks glossy. Remove from the heat. Stir in two thirds of the {{pecorino-romano}} of pecorino Romano. Divide between bowls and top with the remaining pecorino and a generous amount of black pepper.

items.cause: Guanciale and pecorino are both cured and salty; sauce seasoned heavily before the cheese went in
items.fix: Season the sauce lightly during cooking; the pecorino adds significant salt at the end — taste before adding more

items.symptom: Pasta is greasy rather than sauced
items.cause: Pasta water not added, or pasta not tossed vigorously over heat
items.fix: Add pasta water a splash at a time and toss hard over high heat; starch from the water emulsifies the fat into a glossy coating

items.cause: Heat too high or not rendered for long enough
items.fix: Render over medium heat for at least 8 minutes; the fat should be translucent and the meat lightly golden, not soft and raw

content.text: Amatriciana is one of the four canonical Roman pasta sauces, alongside cacio e pepe, carbonara, and gricia. It originates in the Apennine town of Amatrice, 130 km from Rome, where shepherds made a simpler version without tomato. Rome adopted the dish, adapted it, and now claims it as its own — which the Amatriciani dispute. The 2016 earthquake that destroyed much of the old town gave the dispute an edge it did not have before.
